chunks.page_summary: This page details the installation requirements for Rheonics SR-Type viscometers and density sensors (SRV and SRD), focusing on sensing element placement, fluid contact, and considerations for laboratory setups.
chunks.extracted_text:
Installation requirements

The type-SR sensors have a sensing element that requires minimum fluid volume (cross-section) to be maintained as shown in the next image.

rheonlcs
rheonlcs
50 mm
[1.96"]
34 mm
[1.4"]
50 mm
[1.96"]
Ø16 mm
[0.63"]
Ø35 mm
[1.4"]
SRV tip's clearance to any obstruction
5 mm [0.2"]
SRD tip's clearance to any obstruction
12 mm [0.5"]
Avoid stagnation zones in the sensing zones (red areas)
Ensure sensing element in red is in contact with fluid
Figure 1: SRV and SRD sensing areas.

The SRD has an additional requirement relevant to temperature differences greater than 20°C between ambient (sensor probe's back) and fluid. This article (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000412500-maintaining-the-srd-s-temperature-balance-for-high-density-accuracy) explains the topic in detail. This scenario affects the density readings with a systematic offset. The solution is using a long insertion probe or a controlled temperature environment (for the SRD short or flush probes). This last option is more feasible in laboratory setups.

Rheonics offers accessories to use the SR-Type sensors (SRV and SRD) in laboratory setups.

Despite the recommended setup shown in this article, other laboratory installations can be used. Rheonics offers the STCM system (https://rheonics.com/product-accessories/sr-stcm-srv-and-srd-smart-thermal-control-module/) for static or flowing fluids under controlled temperature conditions. Alternatively, clients can use proper setups for their case, following the recommendation made in proper installation of SRV (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000297658) and SRD (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000297659). Next image shows general recommendations for laboratory setups.

General considerations for viscometer SRV measurements in laboratory, static or calibration setup.
key_facts: Type-SR sensors require a minimum fluid volume around their sensing element.
The SRD has an additional requirement regarding temperature differences greater than 20°C between the sensor probe's back and the fluid.
Large temperature differences can cause a systematic offset in SRD density readings.
To address temperature difference issues with SRD, a long insertion probe or a controlled temperature environment is recommended, especially for short or flush probes.
Rheonics provides accessories for using SR-Type sensors (SRV and SRD) in laboratory setups.
The STCM system is offered by Rheonics for managing static or flowing fluids under controlled temperature conditions in laboratory installations.
Clients should follow Rheonics' recommendations for proper installation of SRV and SRD in laboratory setups.
General considerations are available for viscometer SRV measurements in laboratory, static, or calibration setups.
diagram_labels: SRV tip's clearance to any obstruction
SRD tip's clearance to any obstruction
Figure 1: SRV and SRD sensing areas.
warnings_notes: Avoid stagnation zones in the sensing zones (red areas).
Ensure the sensing element (highlighted in red) is in contact with fluid.
Temperature differences greater than 20°C between the SRD sensor probe's back and the fluid can affect density readings with a systematic offset.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides laboratory setup guidelines for Rheonics SRV viscometers and SRD density sensors, detailing fluid recommendations, immersion requirements, temperature considerations, and links to support articles.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 2: SRV Laboratory Setup Guidelines

General considerations for density and viscosity meter SRD measurements in laboratory, static or calibration setup.

Figure 3: SRD Laboratory Setup Guidelines

*If the process fluid temperature differs by more than 15 °C from the ambient temperature, then thermal equilibrium along the probe should be considered, i.e. an oven or a long insertion probe for deeper immersion is recommended.

Read more about SRD’s thermal equilibrium requirement.
(https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000412500-maintaining-the-srd-s-temperature-balance-for-high-density-accuracy)

Review the calibration guidelines article for SRV and SRD.
(https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000390161-calibrating-srv-in-field-verification-and-re-calibration)
key_facts: The document provides setup guidelines for SRV (viscometer) and SRD (density sensor/viscosity meter) in laboratory, static, or calibration environments.
Recommended fluids for measurements include calibrated fluids, oils, and alcohols.
The sensor's sensing area must be fully immersed in the fluid.
Mixing or stirring is optional, but fluid level variations caused by stirring will impact measurement readings.
A heater can be optionally used for testing at different temperatures.

If the process fluid temperature varies more than 15 °C from ambient, thermal equilibrium along the probe should be considered, potentially requiring an oven or a long insertion probe for deeper immersion.
Further details on SRD's thermal equilibrium and SRV/SRD calibration are available on the Rheonics support website.
diagram_labels: Recommended Fluids: - Calibrated fluids - Oils - Alcohols
Make sure the sensing area is fully immersed
Mixing or stirring the fluid is optional. A varying fluid level in sensing area due to stirring will impact the readings
Heater: Optional to test at different temperatures
Heater: Optional to test at near ambient temperature*
warnings_notes: Do not use water (with the sensor in this setup).
A varying fluid level in the sensing area due to stirring will impact the readings.

If the process fluid temperature differs by more than 15 °C from the ambient temperature, thermal equilibrium along the probe should be considered; an oven or a long insertion probe for deeper immersion is recommended.

chunks.page_summary: This page details two types of Rheonics sensor probe stands, the SCS-00A-ME made of stainless steel and the SCS-00A-PM made of POM plastic, describing their materials, recommended uses, and compatibility.
chunks.extracted_text:
Rheonics Sensor Probe Stand

SCS-00A-ME: Stainless Steel Probe Stand

This accessory is made of stainless steel 304 (1.4301), it is an bespoke, robust but light, and high temperature capable stand to firmly hold the sensor probe upwards.

Figure 4: Rheonics Stainless Steel Probe Stand

SCS-00A-PM: POM Plastic Probe Stand

This stand is made of POM (Polyoxymethylene) a high mechanical strength plastic that ensures rigidity in the probe stand. It is recommended for application of up to 65°C only.
Both sensor probe stands, in any material, are the same for both SRV and SRD, hygienic and standard design, EX and EHEDG certified, and all X variants - except the Reactor probe (X7) and slimline probes (X6).

Figure 5: Rheonics POM Plastic Probe Stand

Table 1: Rheonics Probe Stand Setup Example
key_facts: The SCS-00A-ME is a stainless steel 304 probe stand that is robust, light, and high-temperature capable.
The SCS-00A-ME stand is designed to firmly hold the sensor probe upwards.
The SCS-00A-PM stand is made of POM (Polyoxymethylene), a high mechanical strength plastic.
The POM plastic stand ensures rigidity in holding the probe.
Both sensor probe stands (SCS-00A-ME and SCS-00A-PM) are compatible with SRV and SRD sensors.
Both sensor probe stands are compatible with hygienic and standard designs of sensors.
Both sensor probe stands are compatible with EX and EHEDG certified variants of sensors.
Both sensor probe stands are compatible with all X variants of sensors, except for the Reactor probe (X7) and slimline probes (X6).
diagram_labels: Figure 4: Rheonics Stainless Steel Probe Stand
Figure 5: Rheonics POM Plastic Probe Stand
Table 1: Rheonics Probe Stand Setup Example
warnings_notes: The SCS-00A-PM (POM Plastic Probe Stand) is recommended for application of up to 65°C only.
The sensor probe stands are not compatible with the Reactor probe (X7) and slimline probes (X6).

Rheonics Calibration Cylinders - SCC and HCC
These accessory are made of PTFE and are specially designed for the SRV, SRD, SRV-HD or SRD-HD (HD: Hygienic design), also compatible with -HS variants. Due to the SRV's sensing area being smaller than the SRD's, the SRD cylinders can be used for the SRV as well, but not viceversa. All calibration cylinders are listed in next table, they all have a minimum liquid volume required and a maximum allowed, and O-ring required to ensure a leak-free seal. The sensor probe is mounted as shown in the example below.

Figure 6: Rheonics Calibration Cylinder

Table 2: Rheonics Calibration Cylinders
Calibration Cylinder | Designed for [Compatible] | Minimum Volume | Maximum Volume | O-Ring required | Drawing
SCC-SRV | SRV | 19 ml | 21 ml | Ø16x2.5mm | SCC-SRV drawing (https://rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/SCC-SRV.pdf)
SCC-SRD | SRD [SRV] | 50 ml | 66 ml | Ø16x2.5mm | SCC-SRD drawing (https://rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/SCC-SRD.pdf)
key_facts: Rheonics Calibration Cylinders are made of PTFE.
The calibration cylinders are designed for SRV, SRD, SRV-HD, SRD-HD (Hygienic design) probes, and are also compatible with -HS variants.
All calibration cylinders require a minimum liquid volume and an O-ring to ensure a leak-free seal.
The sensor probe is mounted as shown in Figure 6.
Drawings for the Sensor Probe Stand are available at rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Rheonics_SCS-00A-ME.pdf.
The SCC-SRV calibration cylinder is designed for SRV probes.
The SCC-SRD calibration cylinder is designed for SRD probes, and can also be used for SRV probes.
Drawings for the SCC-SRV cylinder are available at rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/SCC-SRV.pdf.
Drawings for the SCC-SRD cylinder are available at rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/SCC-SRD.pdf.
warnings_notes: SRV Sensor Probe variants -X6 and -X7 are not suitable with the listed order codes.
Due to the SRV's sensing area being smaller than the SRD's, the SRD cylinders can be used for the SRV, but not vice versa (SRV cylinders cannot be used for SRD probes).

chunks.page_summary: This page details the components and setup for laboratory calibration of Rheonics viscometers and density sensors, providing specifications for calibration cylinders, probe stands, and sensor probes, along with environmental considerations for accurate measurements.
chunks.extracted_text:
Calibration Cylinder | Designed for [Compatible] | Minimum Volume | Maximum Volume | O-Ring required | Drawing
HCC-SRD | SRD-HD [SRV-HD] | 50 ml | 58 ml | Ø14x2.5mm | HCC-SRD drawing (https://rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/HCC-SRD.pdf)

Calibration setup

Using Rheonics probe stand (SCS-00A-ME) and calibration cylinder (SCC-SRX), the user can create an ideal setup for laboratory uses with static fluid.
The probe stand has holes at the base that can be threaded in tables to avoid unwanted movements.

Figure 7: Calibration stand setup in optical table

Table 3: Laboratory Calibration Setup Example
Description | Order Code | CAD Files
Sensor Probe Stand | SCS-00A-ME | 
SRV Sensor Probe | SRV-X1-34N | SRV Calibration Setup Examples CAD files (https://rheonics.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Rheonics_SR-Calibration-Stand-Setup.zip)
| Suitable for all SRV and SRD probe variants, except -X6 and -X7 | 
Calibration Cylinder | SCC-SRV | 

The setup can be placed in an oven to create a temperature-controlled environment for the measurements. This is especially convenient for the SRD, to keep the thermal balance needed along its resonator and ensure the accuracy in density readings when fluid measurements above or below ambient temperature is required.
key_facts: The Rheonics probe stand (SCS-00A-ME) and calibration cylinder (SCC-SRX) are used to create an ideal setup for laboratory calibration with static fluids.
The probe stand includes threaded holes at its base to secure it to tables, preventing unwanted movements.
The calibration setup can be placed in an oven to create a temperature-controlled environment for measurements.

Temperature control is particularly convenient and important for SRD sensors to maintain thermal balance along their resonators, ensuring accuracy in density readings when fluid measurements are conducted above or below ambient temperature.
The SRV-X1-34N sensor probe is suitable for all SRV and SRD probe variants, with the explicit exception of -X6 and -X7 models.
diagram_labels: Figure 7: Calibration stand setup in optical table
warnings_notes: SRV-X1-34N sensor probe is suitable for all SRV and SRD probe variants, except -X6 and -X7.
For SRD sensors, maintaining thermal balance along its resonator is essential for accurate density readings when fluid measurements are performed above or below ambient temperature.
